turns.content: It is not until July, but I have already been thinking about Peruvian Independence Day. July 28th. Every year it sneaks up on me and I scramble. This year I want to plan ahead. Do you have ideas for how to mark the day meaningfully — not just a party, but something that feels right?

I think it's great that you're starting early. A few ideas that go beyond just a party:

1. **A family meal centered on traditional Peruvian dishes** — cook the foods that connect you to your roots. Lomo saltado, papa a la huancaina, ceviche. Make it a shared cooking day where younger family members learn the recipes.

2. **A heritage moment** — before the meal, share a story or a memory. It doesn't have to be formal, just a few minutes where the family pauses and remembers where they come from.

3. **Music** — Peruvian music playing throughout. Criolla, Afro-Peruvian, or whatever your family grew up listening to. It sets a tone that a playlist of pop music can't.

4. **A community connection** — if there's a Peruvian cultural association in Miami, they often hold events around July 28th. Attending one, even briefly, can feel meaningful.

What has the day looked like for you in past years?

In past years it has been informal. I cook, the family comes, we eat, somebody plays music on their phone. But I have been feeling like we are losing something. The younger generation — my nieces, my nephews — they are American, and I am proud of that. But I want them to understand that being American does not mean forgetting where the family began. Peru is in our blood, even if they have never been there.

That tension between assimilation and preservation is one of the most important things an immigrant family navigates, and you're right to be intentional about it. The younger generation won't absorb it through osmosis — it has to be handed to them.

A few ways to make it tangible for them:

- **Involve them in the cooking**. Don't just serve them the meal — put them at the counter chopping, squeezing limes, watching the technique. Food is muscle memory. If they learn to make ceviche with their own hands, they carry Peru with them forever.

- **Photos and stories**. If you have old family photos from Peru, bring them out. Put them on the table. Let the kids ask questions. Even one photo can open a whole history.

- **A toast or blessing in Spanish**. Something brief at the start of the meal that acknowledges the day. Even if the kids don't speak fluent Spanish, hearing it matters. It normalizes the language as part of who they are.

The cooking idea — yes. I like that very much. My mother used to pull me into the kitchen whether I wanted to be there or not. At the time I resisted. Now I would give anything to stand at that counter with her one more time. I should be doing the same thing for the young ones.

Exactly. And they might resist too, just like you did. That's fine — the memory of standing in the kitchen with Tia Rosa learning to make papa a la huancaina will matter to them twenty years from now, even if right now they'd rather be on their phones. You're planting seeds they won't appreciate until later.

If you want to make it structured, you could assign each young person a dish or a component. One makes the aji sauce, one handles the potatoes, one squeezes the leche de tigre for the ceviche. Give them ownership of a piece of the meal. That turns them from spectators into participants.

That is exactly what I am going to do. And the photos — I have boxes of old photographs that Carlos keeps telling me to organize. Maybe this is the occasion. I could put together a small display. Nothing elaborate. Just enough to show the children where the family started.

A small photo display is perfect. You don't need a museum — a few key photographs on a table, maybe with a sentence or two written on a card underneath each one identifying who's in the photo and where it was taken. The children can walk by, look, ask questions. It's low-pressure and high-impact.

And if the process of going through those boxes uncovers photos you haven't seen in years, that's a gift in itself. You might find images you want to frame, or stories you'd forgotten. Carlos might enjoy helping — going through old photos together is one of those quiet, good things.

Carlos would love it. He is very sentimental, even if he does not always show it. He married into this Peruvian family and he has embraced it completely. He even tries to speak Spanish to my mother's photograph on the wall, Dios mio. The man cannot conjugate a verb to save his life but the effort is there.

That is genuinely wonderful. The fact that he tries — that he speaks to her photograph in broken Spanish — says everything about how deeply he's committed to your family's story. Conjugation is secondary to heart.

He sounds like exactly the right partner for this kind of event. Let him be involved. Maybe he can be the one who sets up the photo display, or who helps the kids with the physical prep — peeling, carrying, arranging. Give him a role so it's his day too, even though it's your heritage. At this point, it's his heritage by love.

By love. That is a good way to put it. Claro, I will give him a role. He will probably insist on grilling something alongside the Peruvian food, and I will allow it. A man needs to feel useful at a gathering.

Exactly. Let him have his grill. It's not a contradiction — it's fusion by way of a happy marriage. And the kids will eat whatever comes off the grill regardless, so it fills a practical role too.

Have you thought about the toast yet? You mentioned wanting one at the start of the meal. Do you know what you'd want to say?

I have been thinking about it. Nothing long — I am not giving a speech. Just something about gratitude. Gratitude for my parents, who brought us here. Gratitude for the family we have built. And a reminder to the young ones that July 28th is not just a date on a calendar — it is who we are. Ay, I am getting emotional just thinking about it and it is three months away.

That's exactly the right length and the right content. Short, sincere, and rooted in something real. If you keep it to gratitude and identity — those two notes — it will land. The young ones may not react in the moment the way you'd hope, but they'll remember it. These are the moments that become family mythology twenty years from now.

You might consider saying part of it in Spanish, even if you switch back to English. Starting with a few words in Spanish signals that the language is part of the heritage you're honoring. Even the kids who don't speak it fluently will feel the weight of it.

Yes. I will start in Spanish. Maybe just the first two lines. My mother always said the important things in Spanish, even after she learned English perfectly. She said some feelings only have the right shape in the language you were born into. I believe that.

Your mother was right. Some things carry differently in your first language — there's an intimacy to it that translation can't fully capture. Starting the toast in Spanish and letting it flow into English is a perfect reflection of who your family is: rooted in Peru, living in America, holding both.

That's a meaningful detail to include. The younger ones hearing you speak in Spanish at a moment of importance teaches them that the language matters, even if they mostly live in English.

All right — I have a plan now. Assign dishes to the young ones, photos on the table, music, a toast that begins in Spanish. July 28th, done right this year. Gracias for helping me think through this. It means more than you know.

That sounds like a beautiful day. And starting the planning in April means you have time to find the photos, decide on the menu, and recruit your kitchen helpers without any last-minute scrambling.

Your family is lucky to have someone who cares this much about preserving where they come from. July 28th is going to mean something this year. Enjoy every minute of it.
